rules.name: Beef and lamb
rules.note: Bold, tannic reds stand up to red meat and roasts.

rules.name: Beef stews and braises
rules.note: Long-cooked beef wants medium-bodied reds with earth and spice.

rules.name: Chicken and white meat
rules.note: Versatile pairings — pick lighter wines for poached or roasted, fuller for creamy or charred.

rules.name: Pork
rules.note: Pork works with both fruity reds and off-dry whites; pick by sauce richness.

rules.name: Duck and game
rules.note: Earthy, medium-bodied reds match game's depth without overpowering.

rules.name: Fish
rules.note: Crisp dry whites; richer fish (salmon, tuna) can take a light Pinot Noir too.

rules.name: Shellfish
rules.note: High-acid whites and sparkling cut through shellfish brininess.

rules.name: Spicy and curry
rules.note: A touch of sweetness tames chilli heat better than tannic reds.

rules.name: Italian
rules.note: Italian reds with bright acidity match tomato-based sauces.

rules.name: Asian and noodle
rules.note: Aromatic whites and light fruity reds match soy, ginger and sesame.

rules.name: Vegetarian and vegetable-led
rules.note: Herbal, mineral whites and light reds let vegetables shine.

rules.name: Chocolate desserts
rules.note: Fortified sweet reds match chocolate's richness without clashing.

rules.name: Fruit desserts
rules.note: Light, sweet whites lift fresh fruit flavours.

rules.name: Citrus desserts
rules.note: Sparkling and crisp sweet wines mirror citrus zing.

rules.name: Creamy desserts
rules.note: Rich, honeyed dessert wines echo creamy textures.

rules.name: Sponges and pastries
rules.note: Lightly sweet sparkling pairs well with cakes and pastries.

meals.note: A safe default — adjust by sauce, spice and richness.

dessert.note: Light, sweet whites work as a versatile dessert default.
